card.title: Growth vs. Fixed Mindset
card.teaser: Two kids get the same test score. One says 'I'm smart'. The other says 'I tried hard'. Decades later, their lives look completely different.
card.coverImagePrompt: A single sapling growing from cracked stone next to a rigid marble statue of a brain. Morning light, painterly, quietly hopeful.

lessons.title: A tale of two praises
lessons.body: In the late 1990s, psychologist Carol Dweck gave 400 New York fifth-graders a simple puzzle. After they finished, half were told 'You must be smart at this', and half 'You must have worked really hard.' Then she offered them a choice: try a harder puzzle, or an easy one again. The kids praised for being smart mostly picked easy. The kids praised for effort overwhelmingly chose the challenge. A single sentence of praise had split them into two different tracks.

visual.imagePrompt: A classroom desk with two small puzzles, one labelled 'easy' and one 'hard', a nervous child's hand hovering between them

lessons.title: Fixed vs. growth
lessons.body: Dweck named the two patterns 'fixed' and 'growth' mindsets. A fixed mindset treats intelligence and talent as stable traits you have or don't have — so failure feels like evidence you lack them. A growth mindset treats them as things you develop through effort — so failure is information about what to try next. It's not about being naturally optimistic. It's about what you believe ability *is*.

keyTerms.definition: The belief that abilities are innate and largely unchangeable
The belief that abilities can be developed through effort and strategy

visual.imagePrompt: Two glass jars side by side — one sealed tight labelled 'fixed', the other open with roots growing out labelled 'growth'

lessons.title: Brains literally respond differently
lessons.body: In follow-up neuroscience work, Moser and Dweck had participants complete tasks while wearing EEG caps. After making a mistake, growth-mindset participants showed a bigger brain response — specifically in an error-correction signal called 'Pe' — and they fixed their errors faster. Fixed-mindset participants' brains essentially looked away. The difference wasn't hypothetical: it was measurable at the millisecond level.
lessons.funFact: Dweck says the single most powerful word you can add to 'I can't do this' is 'yet'.

visual.imagePrompt: An EEG readout with two overlaid waveforms, one reacting strongly to a labelled 'error', the other flat

lessons.title: Why this is not just positive thinking
lessons.body: Recent meta-analyses have been more skeptical. A 2018 review by Sisk and colleagues found growth-mindset interventions had small average effects — though the effects were larger and more reliable for low-income students and struggling learners. In other words, mindset doesn't magically turn anyone into a genius, but for the people furthest from the starting line, it meaningfully changes whether they keep running.

visual.imagePrompt: A research paper underlined with blue pencil, a magnifying glass on top revealing a single sentence in focus

lessons.title: Praising the process
lessons.body: The practical shift is narrow and specific: praise effort, strategy, and learning — not innate ability. 'You figured out a new approach' teaches growth; 'You're so smart' quietly teaches fragility. The same logic applies to self-talk. How you explain your own last failure is probably shaping whether you try the next thing.

quiz.question: In Dweck's puzzle study, which group of kids chose the harder challenge?
quiz.explanation: Praising effort signaled that the challenge itself was what mattered, not protecting a 'smart' identity.

quiz.question: What does a growth mindset fundamentally believe about ability?
options: Ability is developed through effort and strategy
quiz.explanation: Growth mindset frames ability as trainable — failure becomes feedback rather than a verdict.

quiz.question: What did more recent meta-analyses find about growth-mindset interventions?
options: Small overall effects, larger for struggling learners
quiz.explanation: Effects are modest on average but tend to be stronger and more reliable for at-risk learners.

sources.title: Mindset: The New Psychology of Success
To what extent and under which circumstances are growth mindsets important to academic achievement?
